title.zh: 卷五十 張馮汲鄭傳
title.en: Volume 50: Zhang, Feng, Ji and Zheng
title.raw: 卷五十 張馮汲鄭傳 Volume 50: Zhang, Feng, Ji and Zheng

sentences.zh: 卷五十 張馮汲鄭傳第二十

translations.literal: Volume 50, twentieth: Biographies of Zhang, Feng, Ji, and Zheng
translations.idiomatic: Volume 50: The Biographies of Zhang, Feng, Ji, and Zheng (No. 20).

sentences.zh: 張釋之

translations.literal: Zhang Shizhi
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Shizhi

sentences.zh: 張釋之字季，南陽堵陽人也。

translations.literal: Zhang Shizhi, courtesy name Ji, was a native of Duyang in Nanyang commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Shizhi, whose courtesy name was Ji, came from Duyang in Nanyang.

sentences.zh: 與兄仲同居，以貲為騎郎，事文帝，十年不得調，亡所知名。

translations.literal: He dwelt together with his elder brother Zhong; by paying a fee he became a mounted attendant; he served Emperor Wen for ten years without being transferred or promoted, and had no reputation whatever.
translations.idiomatic: He lived with his older brother Zhong, bought his way into the post of mounted attendant, and attended Emperor Wen; for ten years he was never moved to a new post and remained entirely unknown.

sentences.zh: 釋之曰：「久宦減仲之產，不遂。」

translations.literal: Shizhi said, "Long serving as an official has reduced Zhong's property; it has not succeeded."
translations.idiomatic: Shizhi said, "My long service as an official has eaten away at Zhong's estate; nothing has come of it."

sentences.zh: 欲免歸。

translations.literal: He wished to resign and return home.
translations.idiomatic: He wanted to resign and go home.

sentences.zh: 中郎將爰盎知其賢，惜其去，乃請徙釋之補謁者。

translations.literal: The general of gentlemen-at-arms Yuan Ang knew he was worthy, regretted that he would leave, and therefore asked to have Shizhi transferred to fill a vacancy as usher.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Ang, general of the gentlemen-at-arms, knew Shizhi was capable and hated to lose him, so he asked that Shizhi be reassigned to serve as an usher.

sentences.zh: 釋之既朝畢，因前言便宜事。

translations.literal: When Shizhi had finished his court audience, he took the opportunity to speak first of what was expedient.
translations.idiomatic: After court was dismissed, Shizhi stepped forward and spoke of policies that would benefit the state.

sentences.zh: 文帝曰：「卑之，毋甚高論，令今可行也。」

translations.literal: The emperor said, "Keep it modest; do not launch into lofty theories; let what you say be feasible in the present."
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen said, "Keep it practical; spare me lofty theory—tell me what can actually be done today."

sentences.zh: 於是釋之言秦、漢之間事，秦所以失，漢所以興者。

translations.literal: Thereupon Shizhi spoke of affairs between Qin and Han—why Qin lost the realm and why Han rose.
translations.idiomatic: Shizhi then traced events from Qin through to Han, explaining how Qin had lost the empire and how Han had gained it.

sentences.zh: 文帝稱善，拜釋之為謁者僕射。

translations.literal: The emperor praised him and appointed Shizhi chief usher.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor approved, and Shizhi was appointed chief usher (pushe).

sentences.zh: 從行，上登虎圈，問上林尉禽獸簿，十餘問，尉左右視，盡不能對。

translations.literal: On an outing the sovereign ascended the tiger enclosure and questioned the commandant of Shanglin about the register of birds and beasts with more than ten questions; the commandant looked left and right, and none could answer.
translations.idiomatic: During a progress the emperor climbed to the tiger park and put more than ten questions to the Shanglin commandant about the game register; the man looked from side to side and could answer none of them.

sentences.zh: 虎圈嗇夫從旁代尉對上所問禽獸簿甚悉，欲以觀其能口對向應亡窮者。

translations.literal: The overseer of the tiger enclosure, standing by, answered for the commandant everything the sovereign had asked about the register of birds and beasts in great detail, wishing thereby to display one who could answer orally on the spot without limit.
translations.idiomatic: The park overseer at the tiger pen, standing nearby, answered every question about the register in exhaustive detail, hoping to show off a man who could talk his way through anything.

sentences.zh: 文帝曰：「吏不當如此邪？

translations.literal: The emperor said, "Should not officials be like this?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, "Is this not how an official ought to behave?"

sentences.zh: 尉亡賴！」

translations.literal: The commandant is worthless!"
translations.idiomatic: "The commandant is useless!"

sentences.zh: 詔釋之拜嗇夫為上林令。

translations.literal: He ordered Shizhi to appoint the overseer as superintendent of Shanglin.
translations.idiomatic: He told Shizhi to promote that overseer to superintendent of Shanglin.

sentences.zh: 釋之前曰：「陛下以絳侯周勃何如人也？」

translations.literal: Shizhi stepped forward and said, "Your Majesty, what sort of man do you consider Marquis of Jiang Zhou Bo to be?"
translations.idiomatic: Shizhi stepped forward: "Your Majesty, what manner of man do you take Marquis of Jiang Zhou Bo to be?"

sentences.zh: 上曰：「上者。」

translations.literal: The sovereign said, "A superior man."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, "He is a man of substance—a steady elder in conduct."

sentences.zh: 又復問：「東陽侯張相如何如人也？」

translations.literal: He asked again, "What sort of man do you consider Marquis of Dongyang Zhang Xiangru to be?"
translations.idiomatic: He asked again: "And what of Marquis of Dongyang Zhang Xiangru?"

sentences.zh: 上復曰：「長者。」

translations.literal: The sovereign again said, "A senior worthy."
translations.idiomatic: Again the emperor said, "The same—a steady, venerable sort of man."

sentences.zh: 釋之曰：「夫絳侯、東陽侯稱為長者，此兩人言事曾不能出口，豈效此嗇夫喋喋利口捷給哉！

translations.literal: Shizhi said, "When Marquis of Jiang and Marquis of Dongyang are called senior worthies, those two could not even get a word out when discussing affairs—are they to be imitated by this overseer chattering with a sharp tongue and quick answers?
translations.idiomatic: Shizhi replied, "Those two are honored as elder statesmen, yet neither could get a sentence out when state business was discussed. Is Your Majesty to take this overseer, with his endless patter and glib tongue, as the model?"

sentences.zh: 且秦以任刀筆之吏，爭以亟疾苛察相高，其敝徒文具，亡惻隱之實。

translations.literal: Moreover Qin, by entrusting power to brush-and-knife clerks, made them compete to outdo one another in harsh severity and rapid scrutiny; its harm was mere paperwork without the reality of compassion.
translations.idiomatic: Besides, the Qin put petty clerks in charge; they vied to be harsher and quicker to find fault, until government was nothing but paper and the human touch vanished.

sentences.zh: 以故不聞其過，陵夷至於二世，天下土崩。

translations.literal: For that reason the ruler did not hear of his faults, and decline stretched to the Second Generation until all under heaven crumbled.
translations.idiomatic: The ruler therefore never heard his own faults; decline ran on to the Second Emperor, and the realm fell apart.

sentences.zh: 今陛下以嗇夫口辯而超遷之，臣恐天下隨風靡，爭口辯，亡其實。

translations.literal: Now if Your Majesty, because the overseer is eloquent with his mouth, promotes him precipitously, I fear all under heaven will follow the wind and lean that way, striving for glibness and abandoning substance.
translations.idiomatic: If you now leap this man ahead because he talks well, I fear the whole country will take its cue, chase clever words, and forget what is real.

sentences.zh: 且下之化上，疾於景□，舉錯不可不察也。」

translations.literal: Moreover when inferiors take their model from superiors it is swifter than shadow and echo; what you raise up and set down cannot go unexamined."
translations.idiomatic: What those below learn from those above spreads faster than shadow or echo; every move you make in appointments must be weighed with care."

sentences.zh: 文帝曰：「善。」

translations.literal: The emperor said, "Good."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, "Well said."

sentences.zh: 乃止不拜嗇夫。

translations.literal: He therefore desisted and did not appoint the overseer.
translations.idiomatic: He dropped the idea and never gave the overseer the post.

sentences.zh: 就車，召釋之驂乘，徐行，行問釋之秦之敝。

translations.literal: [The emperor] entered his carriage, summoned Shizhi to ride escort beside him, proceeded slowly, and as they went questioned Shizhi about the failings of Qin.
translations.idiomatic: He got into his carriage, had Shizhi take the place beside the driver, and drove slowly, asking as they went what had gone wrong under the Qin.

sentences.zh: 具以質言。

translations.literal: Shizhi reported everything with blunt, substantive answers.
translations.idiomatic: Shizhi answered plainly and to the point.

sentences.zh: 至宮，上拜釋之為公車令。

translations.literal: When they reached the palace, the sovereign appointed Shizhi marshal of the imperial carriage.
translations.idiomatic: Back at the palace the emperor appointed him marshal of the imperial carriage.

sentences.zh: 頃之，太子與梁王共車入朝，不下司馬門，於是釋之追止太子、梁王毋入殿門。

translations.literal: Before long, the heir apparent and the king of Liang shared a carriage into court; they did not dismount at the Sima gate. Shizhi therefore ran after them and stopped the heir apparent and the king of Liang from entering the hall gate.
translations.idiomatic: Soon after, the crown prince and the king of Liang rode together into the palace precincts without alighting at the Sima Gate; Shizhi overtook them and barred them from the inner hall until they complied.

sentences.zh: 遂劾不下公門不敬，奏之。

translations.literal: He thereupon impeached them for failing to dismount at the public gate as an act of disrespect and memorialized it.
translations.idiomatic: He then filed a charge of disrespect for not dismounting at the outer gate and submitted it to the throne.

sentences.zh: 薄太后聞之，文帝免冠謝曰：「教兒子不謹。」

translations.literal: When Empress Dowager Bo heard of it, Emperor Wen removed his cap and apologized, saying, "I failed to instruct my sons with strictness."
translations.idiomatic: When Empress Dowager Bo learned of it, Emperor Wen doffed his cap and apologized: "I was remiss in teaching my sons."

sentences.zh: 薄太后使使承詔赦太子、梁王，然後得入。

translations.literal: Empress Dowager Bo sent an envoy bearing an edict pardoning the crown prince and the king of Liang; only then could they enter.
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager sent messengers with an edict pardoning the crown prince and the king of Liang, after which they were allowed in.

sentences.zh: 文帝繇是奇釋之，拜為中大夫。

translations.literal: From this Emperor Wen thought Shizhi extraordinary and appointed him grand counsellor of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen was struck by Shizhi from that day on and promoted him to grand counsellor of the palace.

sentences.zh: 頃之，至中郎將。

translations.literal: Before long he rose to general of gentlemen-at-arms.
translations.idiomatic: In due course he rose to general of the gentlemen-at-arms.

sentences.zh: 從行至霸陵，上居外臨廁。

translations.literal: On progress they reached Baling; the sovereign stopped outside and leaned over the privy.
translations.idiomatic: On a progress to Baling the emperor halted outside and stepped to the privy.

sentences.zh: 時慎夫人從，上指視慎夫人新豐道，曰：「此走邯鄲道也。」

translations.literal: At that time Lady Shen was in attendance; the sovereign pointed out to Lady Shen the road to Xinfeng and said, "This is the road that runs to Handan."
translations.idiomatic: Lady Shen was with him; he pointed toward the Xinfeng road for her and said, "That is the road to Handan."

sentences.zh: 使慎夫人鼓瑟，上自倚瑟而歌，意淒愴悲懷，顧謂群臣曰：「嗟乎！

translations.literal: He had Lady Shen strum the se; the sovereign himself leaned on the se and sang, his mood mournful and sorrowful, and turning to the ministers he said, "Alas!
translations.idiomatic: He had Lady Shen play the se and himself sang along, leaning on the instrument, his heart full of grief; turning to the ministers he said, "Ah,

sentences.zh: 以北山石為槨，用□絮斫陳漆其間，豈可動哉！」

translations.literal: If we take the stone north of the mountain for the outer coffin, and pack floss in layers and lacquer the gaps between, could anything disturb it!"
translations.idiomatic: if we used the northern mountain for an outer shell and filled the joints with packed floss and lacquer, who could ever breach it!"

sentences.zh: 左右皆曰：「善。」

translations.literal: Those on his left and right all said, "Good."
translations.idiomatic: Those at his side said, "Indeed."

sentences.zh: 釋之前曰：「使其中有可欲，雖錮南山猶有隙；

translations.literal: Shizhi stepped forward and said, "If there is still within it something people might covet, though you seal off the South Mountain there would still be a crack;
translations.idiomatic: Shizhi stepped forward: "If there is anything inside worth stealing, sealing it with the whole Southern Range would still leave a crack;

sentences.zh: 使其中亡可欲，雖亡石槨，又何戚焉？」

translations.literal: if there is nothing within worth coveting, even without a stone outer coffin, what grief would there be?"
translations.idiomatic: if there is nothing worth taking, then even without a stone sarcophagus, what is there to fear?"

sentences.zh: 文帝稱善。

translations.literal: The emperor praised him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor praised his answer.

sentences.zh: 其後，拜釋之為廷尉。

translations.literal: After that he appointed Shizhi commandant of justice.
translations.idiomatic: He later appointed Shizhi commandant of justice (tingwei).

sentences.zh: 頃之，上行出中渭橋，有一人從橋下走，乘輿馬驚。

translations.literal: Before long, on a progress the sovereign was leaving by the Zhongwei Bridge when a man ran from under the bridge and startled the horses of the imperial equipage.
translations.idiomatic: Not long after, the emperor was crossing the middle Wei Bridge when someone darted from beneath it and spooked the imperial horses.

sentences.zh: 於是使騎捕之，屬廷尉。

translations.literal: Thereupon he had horsemen seize him and turn him over to the commandant of justice.
translations.idiomatic: Horsemen were sent to seize the man and hand him to the commandant of justice.

sentences.zh: 釋之治問。

translations.literal: Shizhi tried and questioned him.
translations.idiomatic: Shizhi examined him under the law.

sentences.zh: 曰：「縣人來，聞蹕，匿橋下。

translations.literal: He said, "I am a man of the county who came; hearing the clearing of the way I hid under the bridge.
translations.idiomatic: The man said, "I am a commoner from the countryside. I heard the procession clearing the road and hid under the bridge."

sentences.zh: 久，以為行過，既出，見車騎，即走耳。」

translations.literal: After a long time I thought the procession had passed; when I came out and saw the chariots and riders I ran—that is all."
translations.idiomatic: I waited so long I thought the train had gone; when I stepped out and saw the escort I ran—that is all there is to it."

sentences.zh: 釋之奏當：「此人犯蹕，當罰金。」

translations.literal: Shizhi reported the appropriate verdict: "This person violated the clearing; the penalty is a fine in gold."
translations.idiomatic: Shizhi reported the statutory sentence: "This man broke the law on clearing the way; the fine is in gold."

sentences.zh: 上怒曰：「此人親驚吾馬，馬賴和柔，令它馬，固不敗傷我乎？

translations.literal: The emperor angrily said, "He personally startled my horses; thanks to their mild temper, had it been other horses would they not surely have injured me?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor flared: "He nearly threw my horses; they were gentle—another team might have killed me."

sentences.zh: 而廷尉乃當之罰金！」

translations.literal: Yet the commandant would match that with a fine in gold!"
translations.idiomatic: And you answer with a mere fine!"

sentences.zh: 釋之曰：「法者，天子所與天下公共也。

translations.literal: Shizhi said, "Law is what the Son of Heaven shares in common with all under heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Shizhi said, "The law is something Your Majesty holds in common with every subject."

sentences.zh: 今法如是，更重之，是法不信於民也。

translations.literal: Now the law is thus; to make it heavier would be for the law not to be trusted by the people.
translations.idiomatic: The statute says what it says; to stretch it heavier would teach the people not to trust the law.

sentences.zh: 且方其時，上使使誅之則已。

translations.literal: Moreover at the moment had Your Majesty sent someone to execute him it would have been over.
translations.idiomatic: Besides, at the moment it happened you could have sent a man to behead him and been done.

sentences.zh: 今已下廷尉，廷尉，天下之平也，壹傾，天下用法皆為之輕重，民安所錯其手足？

translations.literal: Now that it has come down to the commandant of justice—the commandant of justice is the balance for all the world—once it tips one way, everywhere under heaven application of the law would grow light or heavy accordingly; where are the people to place their hands and feet?
translations.idiomatic: Now it has reached the commandant of justice—and he is the level for the whole realm. Tip that balance once, and every magistrate will bend sentences up or down; where are ordinary folk to put their hands and feet?

sentences.zh: 唯陛下察之。」

translations.literal: I beg Your Majesty to examine this."
translations.idiomatic: I beg you to think this through."

sentences.zh: 上良久曰：「廷尉當是也。」

translations.literal: After a long silence the sovereign said, "The commandant's verdict is correct."
translations.idiomatic: After a long silence the emperor said, "The commandant is right."

sentences.zh: 其後人有盜高廟座前玉環，得，文帝怒，下廷尉治。

translations.literal: Later someone stole a jade ring from before the seat in Gaozu's temple; he was caught and Emperor Wen, furious, handed him to the commandant of justice for trial.
translations.idiomatic: Later someone stole a jade ring from the dais in Gaozu's temple; when he was caught Emperor Wen, in a rage, sent him to the commandant of justice.

sentences.zh: 案盜宗廟服御物者為奏，當棄市。

translations.literal: When the case of one who stole ritual objects from the ancestral temple was reported, the matched penalty was public execution in the market.
translations.idiomatic: Shizhi judged theft of imperial ritual gear from the ancestral shrines and recommended execution in the marketplace.

sentences.zh: 上大怒曰：「人亡道，乃盜先帝器！

translations.literal: The emperor said in great anger, "That man lacks the way; he stole the late emperor's vessel!
translations.idiomatic: The emperor thundered, "The man is utterly depraved—he stole an object of the late emperor!

sentences.zh: 吾屬廷尉者，欲致之族，而君以法奏之，非吾所以共承宗廟意也。」

translations.literal: I handed him to the commandant of justice wishing to have his kin exterminated; yet you memorialized according to the law—that is not how I meant to share in sustaining the ancestral temple."
translations.idiomatic: I sent him to you expecting the extermination of his clan; instead you cite the statutes. That is not how I meant to uphold the shrines of our house."

sentences.zh: 釋之免冠頓首謝曰：「法如是足也。

translations.literal: Shizhi removed his cap and knocked his head in apology, saying, "According to the law this is sufficient punishment.
translations.idiomatic: Shizhi doffed his cap and kowtowed: "The law already prescribes the utmost for this offense.

sentences.zh: 且罪等，然以逆順為基。

translations.literal: Moreover when crimes are equal, the judgment takes orderly versus rebellious conduct as its basis.
translations.idiomatic: When crimes are of the same grade, one still weighs whether the act was calculated rebellion or mere disorder.

sentences.zh: 今盜宗廟器而族之，有如萬分一，假令愚民取長陵一□土，陛下且何以加其法乎？」

translations.literal: Now to exterminate a whole clan for stealing temple vessels—if there were one chance in ten thousand, suppose common folk took one handful of earth from Changling—how would Your Majesty add to the law then?"
translations.idiomatic: To wipe out a clan for stealing from the temple—if there were the slightest chance some fool took a handful of soil from your own Changling mound, what heavier penalty could you add?

sentences.zh: 文帝與太后言之，乃許廷尉當。

translations.literal: The emperor spoke of it with the empress dowager and then approved the commandant's verdict.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor took the matter to the empress dowager and then accepted the commandant's sentence.

sentences.zh: 是時，中尉條侯周亞夫與梁相山都侯王恬啟見釋之持議平，乃結為親友。

translations.literal: At that time Commandant Zhou Yafu of the Tiao marquisate and Wang Tianqi, marquis of Shandu and minister of Liang, saw that Shizhi held opinions fairly; they therefore formed friendly ties with him.
translations.idiomatic: At the time Commandant Zhou Yafu of the Tiao marquisate and Wang Tianqi, marquis of Shandu and minister of Liang, saw how fairly Shizhi decided cases and became his close friends.

sentences.zh: 張廷尉繇此天下稱之。

translations.literal: Commandant Zhang was praised throughout the realm for this.
translations.idiomatic: From then on Commandant Zhang was honored across the empire.

sentences.zh: 文帝崩，景帝立，釋之恐，稱疾。

translations.literal: When Emperor Wen died and Emperor Jing took the throne, Shizhi was afraid and pleaded illness.
translations.idiomatic: When Wen died and Jing succeeded, Shizhi grew fearful and pleaded illness.

sentences.zh: 欲免去，懼大誅至；

translations.literal: He wished to resign and leave but feared execution on a great scale would come;
translations.idiomatic: He wanted to resign yet dreaded a sweeping punishment;

sentences.zh: 欲見，則未知何如。

translations.literal: he wished to appear but did not know how it would go.
translations.idiomatic: he thought of facing the new emperor but could not tell how he would be received.

sentences.zh: 用王生計，卒見謝，景帝不過也。

translations.literal: Following Wang Sheng's plan he finally went in to apologize, and Emperor Jing did not fault him beyond measure.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Sheng's advice won out: he went in to apologize, and Emperor Jing did not press the old grudge too far.

sentences.zh: 王生者，善為黃、老言，處士。

translations.literal: Wang Sheng was skilled at discourse in the Huang-Lao tradition and was a recluse.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Sheng was a recluse who excelled at Huang-Lao teaching.

sentences.zh: 嘗召居廷中，公卿盡會立。

translations.literal: He was once summoned to reside in the palace when the dukes and ministers had all met and stood in order.
translations.idiomatic: Once he was called to wait inside the palace while the high ministers stood assembled.

sentences.zh: 王生老人，曰「吾襪解」，顧謂釋之：「為我結襪！」

translations.literal: Wang Sheng, an old man, said "My leg bands have come loose," and looking toward Shizhi said, "Tie my leg bands for me!"
translations.idiomatic: This old man suddenly said, "My leggings have slipped," and told Shizhi, "Tie them for me!"

sentences.zh: 釋之跪而結之，既已，人或讓王生：「獨奈何廷辱張廷尉如此？」

translations.literal: Shizhi knelt and tied them; when it was done someone reproached Wang Sheng: "How could you alone thus humiliate Commandant Zhang in court?"
translations.idiomatic: Shizhi knelt and fastened them. Afterwards someone scolded Wang Sheng: "Why humiliate Commandant Zhang like that in open court?"

sentences.zh: 王生曰：「吾老且賤，自度終亡益於張廷尉。

translations.literal: Wang Sheng said, "I am old and of lowly station; I reckon I can never benefit Commandant Zhang in the end.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Sheng said, "I am old and obscure; I will never advance Zhang's career by my own weight.

sentences.zh: 廷尉方天下名臣，吾故聊使結襪，欲以重之。」

translations.literal: The commandant of justice is the empire's famed minister; I deliberately had him tie my leg bands for a moment wishing thereby to give him added weight."
translations.idiomatic: He is already the realm's most celebrated judge; I meant only to lend him a little extra dignity by having him do this."

sentences.zh: 諸公聞之，賢王生而重釋之。

translations.literal: When the dukes heard this they thought Wang Sheng worthy and held Shizhi in higher regard.
translations.idiomatic: The nobles heard the story, admired Wang Sheng's tact, and respected Shizhi all the more.

sentences.zh: 釋之事景帝歲餘，為淮南相，猶尚以前過也。

translations.literal: Shizhi served Emperor Jing for over a year as chancellor of Huainan, still on account of the earlier offense.
translations.idiomatic: After a little more than a year serving Jing, Shizhi was made chancellor of Huainan—still a demotion tied to the earlier incident at the gate.

sentences.zh: 年老病卒。

translations.literal: He died of old age and illness.
translations.idiomatic: He died in old age of natural causes.

sentences.zh: 其子摯，字長公，官至大夫，免。

translations.literal: His son Zhi, courtesy name Zhangong, rose to the rank of grandee but was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: His son Zhi, courtesy name Zhangong, reached the rank of grandee before being dismissed.

sentences.zh: 以不能取容當世，故終身不仕。

translations.literal: Because he could not accommodate himself to the age he never served in office all his life.
translations.idiomatic: Unable to trim his sails to the times, he never held office again.

sentences.zh: 馮唐，祖父趙人也。

translations.literal: Feng Tang; his grandfather was a man of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Feng Tang's grandfather was a native of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 父徙代。

translations.literal: His father moved to Dai.
translations.idiomatic: His father later resettled in Dai.

sentences.zh: 漢興徙安陵。

translations.literal: When Han rose they moved to Anling.
translations.idiomatic: After the Han founding the family moved to Anling.

sentences.zh: 唐以孝著，為郎中署長，事文帝。

translations.literal: Tang was noted for filial piety; he was chief of a section in the office of palace gentlemen and served Emperor Wen.
translations.idiomatic: Feng Tang was known for filial devotion; he headed a desk in the gentlemen's office and served under Emperor Wen.

sentences.zh: 帝輦過，問唐曰：「父老何自為郎？

translations.literal: When the imperial palanquin passed by, [the emperor] asked Tang, "Elder, how did you yourself become a gentleman-at-court?"
translations.idiomatic: As the imperial litter passed, the emperor called out, "Old sir, how is it that you yourself hold the post of gentleman-at-court?"

sentences.zh: 家安在？」

translations.literal: "Where is your home?"
translations.idiomatic: "Where is your household?"

sentences.zh: 具以實言。

translations.literal: Tang answered with the full truth.
translations.idiomatic: Feng Tang told him the whole story plainly.

sentences.zh: 文帝曰：「吾居代時，吾尚食監高祛數為我言趙將李齊之賢，戰於鉅鹿下。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen said, "When I dwelt in Dai my commissioner of palace provender Gao Qu often spoke to me of the worth of Zhao's general Li Qi and how he fought below Julu.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen said, "In my days in Dai my palace provender Gao Qu used to praise Zhao's general Li Qi and his fighting below Julu."

sentences.zh: 吾每飲食，意未嘗不在鉅鹿也。

translations.literal: Whenever I eat and drink my thoughts have never not been at Julu.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever I dine, my mind is still there at Julu.

sentences.zh: 父老知之乎？」

translations.literal: Do you know of that, elder?"
translations.idiomatic: Do you know that story, old sir?"

sentences.zh: 唐對曰：「齊尚不如廉頗、李牧之為將也。」

translations.literal: Tang replied, "As a general Li Qi cannot yet match Lian Po or Li Mu."
translations.idiomatic: Feng Tang answered, "Li Qi was no match, as a commander, for Lian Po or Li Mu."

sentences.zh: 上曰：「何已？」

translations.literal: The sovereign said, "What of it?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, "How so?"

sentences.zh: 唐曰：「臣大父在趙時，為官帥將，善李牧。

translations.literal: Tang said, "When my grandfather was in Zhao he led troops as a commandery officer and was close to Li Mu.
translations.idiomatic: Feng Tang said, "My grandfather served in Zhao as a commandery officer commanding troops and was on close terms with Li Mu.

sentences.zh: 臣父故為代相，善李齊，知其為人也。」

translations.literal: My late father formerly was chancellor of Dai and was close to Li Qi; I know what kind of man he was."
translations.idiomatic: My late father was chancellor of Dai and knew Li Qi well; I understand what sort of general he was."

sentences.zh: 上既聞廉頗、李牧為人，良說，乃拊髀曰：「嗟乎！

translations.literal: Having heard how Lian Po and Li Mu were as men, [the emperor] was greatly pleased and slapped his thigh, saying, "Alas!
translations.idiomatic: Learning what manner of men Lian Po and Li Mu had been, the emperor was delighted; he slapped his thigh and cried, "Ah,

sentences.zh: 吾獨不得廉頗、李牧為將，豈憂匈奴哉！」

translations.literal: If only I could obtain Lian Po and Li Mu as generals, would I need to worry about the Xiongnu!"
translations.idiomatic: if only I had Lian Po and Li Mu to lead my armies—would I still lose sleep over the Xiongnu!"

sentences.zh: 唐曰：「主臣！

translations.literal: Tang said, "Lord—your minister!"
translations.idiomatic: Tang said, "Your Majesty, your servant spoke out of turn—I am awestruck at my own presumption!"

sentences.zh: 陛下雖有廉頗、李牧，不能用也。」

translations.literal: "Though Your Majesty had Lian Po and Li Mu, you could not employ them."
translations.idiomatic: "Even with men like Lian Po and Li Mu in the realm, Your Majesty could not actually use them."

sentences.zh: 上怒，起入禁中。

translations.literal: The emperor grew angry, rose, and went into the inner apartments.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor flared up, stood, and withdrew to the private quarters.

sentences.zh: 良久，召唐讓曰：「公眾辱我，獨亡間處乎？」

translations.literal: After a long while he summoned Tang and rebuked him: "You have publicly humiliated me—have you no place where we could have spoken alone?"
translations.idiomatic: When he finally called Feng Tang back, he scolded him: "Why shame me in front of everyone? Could we not have had this conversation in private?"

sentences.zh: 唐謝曰：「鄙人不知忌諱。」

translations.literal: Tang apologized, saying, "This rustic did not know what was forbidden."
translations.idiomatic: Feng Tang bowed: "I am a blunt countryman; I did not know the taboos."

sentences.zh: 當是時，匈奴新大入朝那，殺北地都尉卬。

translations.literal: At that time the Xiongnu had recently made a major incursion into Chaona, killing Ang, commandant of Beidi.
translations.idiomatic: Just then the Xiongnu had struck deep into Chaona and slain Sun Ang, the commandant of Beidi.

sentences.zh: 上以胡寇為意，乃卒復問唐曰：「公何以言吾不能用頗、牧也？」

translations.literal: The sovereign had the Hu raiders on his mind and therefore finally asked Tang again, "By what do you say I could not employ Po and Mu?"
translations.idiomatic: With the barbarian threat weighing on him, the emperor circled back: "What did you mean when you said I could not use Lian Po and Li Mu even if I had them?"

sentences.zh: 唐對曰，「臣聞上古王者遣將也，跪而推轂，曰：『□以內寡人制之，□以外將軍制之；

translations.literal: Tang replied, "I have heard that when the high kings of antiquity dispatched a general they knelt and pushed the chariot hub, saying, 'Within the [gate] I control it; outside the [gate] the general controls it;
translations.idiomatic: Feng Tang answered, "The old stories say that when a true king sent out an army he knelt to push the wheels and told the commander, 'What lies inside the capital is mine; what lies beyond the passes is yours;

sentences.zh: 軍功爵賞，皆決於外，歸而奏之。』

translations.literal: military merit, noble ranks, and rewards were all decided outside and only then reported on return.'
translations.idiomatic: promotions, titles, and bonuses were decided in the field and reported afterward.'

sentences.zh: 此非空言也。

translations.literal: This was not empty talk.
translations.idiomatic: That was not hollow rhetoric.

sentences.zh: 臣大父言李牧之為趙將居邊，軍市之租皆自用饗士，賞賜決於外，不從中復也。

translations.literal: My grandfather said that when Li Mu was Zhao's general holding the frontier, the levies from the army market he used entirely himself to feast the troops; rewards and gifts were decided outside and were not re-checked from the center.
translations.idiomatic: My grandfather told me how Li Mu, as Zhao's frontier commander, spent every coin of the army-market tax to feed his men and handed out rewards on his own authority, without waiting for approval from Xianyang.

sentences.zh: 委任而責成功，故李牧乃得盡其知能，選車千三百乘，彀騎萬三千匹，百金之士十萬，是以北逐單于，破東胡，滅澹林，西抑強秦，南支韓、魏。

translations.literal: He was entrusted with responsibility and held to success; therefore Li Mu was able to use all his knowledge and ability—he selected thirteen hundred war chariots, thirteen thousand mounted archers, a hundred thousand men worth a hundred in gold—and so drove the Chanyu northward, broke the Eastern Hu, destroyed the Danlin, checked mighty Qin to the west, and propped up Han and Wei to the south.
translations.idiomatic: Given full trust and answerable only for results, Li Mu could deploy his genius: thirteen hundred chariots, thirteen thousand horse archers, and a hundred thousand picked warriors—enough to chase the Chanyu, crush the Eastern Hu, wipe out the Danlin, hold Qin at bay, and shield Han and Wei on the south.

sentences.zh: 當是時，趙幾伯。

translations.literal: At that time Zhao was almost hegemon among the states.
translations.idiomatic: For a time Zhao nearly ranked as first among the powers.

sentences.zh: 後會趙王遷立，其母倡也，用郭開讒，而誅李牧，令顏聚代之。

translations.literal: Later, when King Qian of Zhao came to the throne—his mother had been a singing-girl—she employed Guo Kai's slander, executed Li Mu, and ordered Yan Ju to replace him.
translations.idiomatic: Then King Qian of Zhao ascended; his mother was a former singing girl who listened to Guo Kai, had Li Mu put to death, and put Yan Ju in his place.

sentences.zh: 是以為秦所滅。

translations.literal: Therefore Zhao was destroyed by Qin.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao fell to Qin because of it.

sentences.zh: 今臣竊聞魏尚為雲中守，軍市租盡以給士卒，出私養錢，五日壹殺牛，以饗賓客軍吏舍人，是以匈奴遠避，不近雲中之塞。

translations.literal: Now I have privately heard that Wei Shang as governor of Yunzhong spent all the army-market tax to supply the soldiers, paid out his own money, slaughtered an ox every five days to feast guests, officers, and retainers—therefore the Xiongnu kept their distance and did not approach the Yunzhong frontier.
translations.idiomatic: I hear that Wei Shang, as governor of Yunzhong, poured every penny of the army-market revenue into his troops, dug into his own purse, and butchered a steer every five days for his staff—so the Xiongnu gave the Yunzhong walls a wide berth.

sentences.zh: 虜嘗一入，尚帥車騎擊之，所殺甚眾。

translations.literal: When the raiders once entered, Shang led chariots and cavalry against them and killed very many.
translations.idiomatic: On the one raid they tried, Shang led horse and chariot against them and piled up a heavy butcher's bill.

sentences.zh: 夫士卒盡家人子，起田中從軍，安知尺籍伍符？

translations.literal: Now the rank and file are mostly sons of families, raised in the fields and drafted into the army—how should they know tally-rosters and five-man squad tallies?
translations.idiomatic: These soldiers are farm boys called up from the plough; how are they to master every line of the regulations and squad tallies?

sentences.zh: 終日力戰，斬首捕虜，上功莫府，一言不相應，文吏以法繩之。

translations.literal: They fight with all their strength all day, behead enemies and take captives, report merit to the commandant's headquarters—if one word fails to match, the clerkly officials bind them with the statutes.
translations.idiomatic: They spend the day in desperate combat, then send head-counts to headquarters; one slip in the paperwork and some desk-bound judge nails them to the letter of the law.

sentences.zh: 其賞不行，吏奉法必用。

translations.literal: Their rewards do not go through; the clerks invariably apply the law to the full.
translations.idiomatic: Their battle pay never arrives, while the law officers enforce every comma.

sentences.zh: 愚以為陛下法太明，賞太輕，罰太重。

translations.literal: In my foolish view Your Majesty's laws are too clear, the rewards too slight, the punishments too heavy.
translations.idiomatic: Forgive my bluntness, but I believe your code is too rigid, your rewards too stingy, and your penalties too harsh.

sentences.zh: 且雲中守尚坐上功首虜差六級，陛下下之吏，削其爵，罰作之。

translations.literal: Moreover the governor of Yunzhong, Shang, sat in judgment for overstating his beheaded captives by six grades; Your Majesty handed him to the clerks, stripped his rank, and sentenced him to labor.
translations.idiomatic: Take Wei Shang: he overstated six heads in his battle report; you sent him to the judges, stripped his nobility, and set him to hard labor.

sentences.zh: 繇此言之，陛下雖得李牧，不能用也。

translations.literal: From this it follows that though Your Majesty obtained Li Mu, you could not employ him.
translations.idiomatic: Put that together and even Li Mu could not have served you under such rules.

sentences.zh: 臣誠愚，觸忌諱，死罪！」

translations.literal: I am truly foolish; I have touched on forbidden matters—crime deserving death!"
translations.idiomatic: I have been a fool and broached what I should not—worthy of death!"

sentences.zh: 文帝說。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen was pleased.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen's anger melted into approval.

sentences.zh: 是日，令唐持節赦魏尚，復以為雲中守，而拜唐為車騎都尉，主中尉及郡國車士。

translations.literal: That same day he ordered Tang to take the staff of authority and pardon Wei Shang, restoring him as governor of Yunzhong, and appointed Tang chief commandant of chariots and cavalry, in charge of the commandant of the capital and chariot troops of the commanderies and kingdoms.
translations.idiomatic: That very day he sent Feng Tang with the imperial baton to free Wei Shang and reinstate him at Yunzhong, while Feng Tang himself was made chief commandant of chariots and cavalry, overseeing the metropolitan commandant and mounted forces from the provinces and principalities.

sentences.zh: 汲黯字長孺，濮陽人也。

translations.literal: Ji An, courtesy name Zhangru, was a native of Puyang.
translations.idiomatic: Ji An, whose courtesy name was Zhangru, came from Puyang.

sentences.zh: 其行有寵於古之衛君也。

translations.literal: His conduct had favor with the ancient lord of Wei.
translations.idiomatic: In his bearing he resembled the old lords of Wei who won the people's love.

sentences.zh: 至黯十世，世為卿大夫。

translations.literal: Down to An, for ten generations they had been ministers and grandees.
translations.idiomatic: For ten generations before him the family had filled ministerial posts.

sentences.zh: 以父任，孝景時為太子洗馬，以嚴見憚。

translations.literal: By his father's privilege, under Emperor Jing he became groom of the heir apparent and was feared for his severity.
translations.idiomatic: Recommended on his father's record, he served Emperor Jing as groom to the crown prince and was dreaded for his uncompromising sternness.

sentences.zh: 武帝即位，黯為謁者。

translations.literal: When Emperor Wu took the throne, An became an usher.
translations.idiomatic: When Wu succeeded, Ji An was appointed an usher.

sentences.zh: 東粵相攻，上使黯往視之。

translations.literal: The Eastern Yue states attacked one another; the sovereign sent An to inspect.
translations.idiomatic: When the Eastern Yue principalities turned on each other, the emperor dispatched Ji An to look into it.

sentences.zh: 至吳而還，報曰：「粵人相攻，固其俗，不足以辱天子使者。」

translations.literal: He turned back at Wu and reported, "The Yue people fighting one another is their custom; it is not worth troubling an envoy of the Son of Heaven."
translations.idiomatic: He got only as far as Wu and sent word back: "Let the Yue fight among themselves—it is their habit and hardly merits an imperial envoy."

sentences.zh: 河內失火，燒千餘家，上使黯往視之。

translations.literal: A fire in Henei burned more than a thousand households; the sovereign sent An to inspect.
translations.idiomatic: When fire ravaged a thousand homes in Henei, the emperor sent him again.

sentences.zh: 還報曰：「家人失火，屋比延燒，不足憂。

translations.literal: He returned and reported, "A common household fire spread along adjoining roofs; there is nothing to worry about.
translations.idiomatic: His report read: "It was an ordinary house fire that jumped roof to roof—nothing for the court to fret over."

sentences.zh: 臣過河內，河內貧人傷水旱萬餘家，或父子相食，臣謹以便宜，持節發河內倉粟以振貧民。

translations.literal: Passing through Henei I saw more than ten thousand poor harmed by flood and drought, some fathers and sons eating one another; I dared, as expedient, to take the staff and open the Henei granaries to relieve the poor.
translations.idiomatic: But on my way through Henei I found tens of thousands starving from flood and drought, some families reduced to cannibalism; I used my commission to open the government granaries and feed them.

sentences.zh: 請歸節，伏矯制罰。」

translations.literal: I beg to return the staff and submit to punishment for counterfeiting an order."
translations.idiomatic: I now return the baton and ask to be punished for forging an imperial order.

sentences.zh: 上賢而釋之，遷為滎陽令。

translations.literal: The sovereign thought him worthy and released him, promoting him to magistrate of Xingyang.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor judged him loyal, forgave the offense, and named him magistrate of Xingyang.

sentences.zh: 黯恥為令，稱疾歸田裡。

translations.literal: An was ashamed to be a magistrate and pleaded illness, returning to his village fields.
translations.idiomatic: Ji An found a county post beneath him, feigned illness, and went home to his fields.

sentences.zh: 上聞，乃召為中大夫。

translations.literal: When the sovereign heard of it, he summoned him to be grand counsellor of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: When word reached the throne, Wu recalled him as grand counsellor of the palace.

sentences.zh: 以數切諫，不得久留內，遷為東海太守。

translations.literal: Because he remonstrated sharply and repeatedly, he could not long remain inside and was transferred to governor of the Eastern Sea.
translations.idiomatic: His blunt memorials made him unwelcome at court, so he was shifted to governor of Donghai.

sentences.zh: 黯學黃、老言，治官民，好清靜，擇丞史任之，責大指而已，不細苛。

translations.literal: An studied Huang-Lao teaching; in governing officials and commoners he favored quietude, chose assistants and clerks to carry the burden, and insisted only on the main points, not petty harshness.
translations.idiomatic: Ji An followed Huang-Lao principles: he ran his command with a light hand, delegated to capable deputies, and cared about broad outcomes rather than petty rules.

sentences.zh: 黯多病，臥閣內不出。

translations.literal: An was often ill and lay in his quarters without coming out.
translations.idiomatic: Plagued by illness, he rarely left his inner office.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，東海大治，稱之。

translations.literal: After more than a year the Eastern Sea was greatly well ordered and he was praised.
translations.idiomatic: Within a year Donghai was models of order and the people sang his praise.

sentences.zh: 上聞，召為主爵都尉，列於九卿。

translations.literal: When the sovereign heard of it, he summoned him to be chief commandant of chief rank for nobles, ranking with the nine ministers.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor then brought him back as chief commandant for noble ranks—one of the nine high ministers.

sentences.zh: 治務在無為而已，引大體，不拘文法。

translations.literal: His administration aimed at non-action; he cited the larger pattern and was not bound by written formulas.
translations.idiomatic: His style was wuwei in the best sense: he set the tone and let the law follow common sense.

sentences.zh: 為人性倨，少禮，面折，不能容人之過。

translations.literal: By nature he was proud, short on ceremony, confronted others to their face, and could not tolerate others' faults.
translations.idiomatic: He was haughty, scanted ritual, spoke his mind to men's faces, and brooked no foolishness.

sentences.zh: 合己者善待之，不合者弗能忍見，士亦以此不附焉。

translations.literal: Those who suited him he treated well; those who did not he could not bear to see—gentlemen therefore did not flock to him.
translations.idiomatic: He warmed to allies and could hardly stand anyone else, so men of talent kept their distance.

sentences.zh: 然好遊俠，任氣節，行修潔。

translations.literal: Yet he loved wandering knights, trusted to spirit and integrity, and conducted himself with clean discipline.
translations.idiomatic: Yet he admired knights-errant, honored personal honor, and kept his own hands clean.

sentences.zh: 其諫，犯主之顏色。

translations.literal: In remonstrance he offended the ruler's expression.
translations.idiomatic: When he remonstrated he looked the emperor in the eye and contradicted him.

sentences.zh: 常慕傅伯、爰盎之為人。

translations.literal: He always admired the conduct of Fu Bo and Yuan Ang.
translations.idiomatic: He modeled himself on Fu Bo and Yuan Ang.

sentences.zh: 善灌夫、鄭當時及宗正劉棄疾。

translations.literal: He was on good terms with Guan Fu, Zheng Dangshi, and Liu Qiji, director of the imperial clan.
translations.idiomatic: He counted Guan Fu, Zheng Dangshi, and the director of the imperial clan Liu Qiji among his friends.

sentences.zh: 亦以數直諫，不得久居位。

translations.literal: Also because he remonstrated straight repeatedly, he could not long hold office.
translations.idiomatic: His habit of blunt truth-telling kept him from staying long in any post.

sentences.zh: 是時，太后弟武安侯田□分為丞相，中二千石拜謁，□分弗為禮。

translations.literal: At that time the empress dowager's younger brother, Marquis of Wu'an Tian Fen, was chancellor; officials at two thousand piculs paid their respects, yet Fen did not return the courtesy.
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile Tian Fen, the empress dowager's brother and Marquis of Wu'an, sat as chancellor; ministers at the two-thousand-picul rank bowed to him, and he did not bother to bow back.

sentences.zh: 黯見□分，未嘗拜，揖之。

translations.literal: When An saw Fen he never bowed but merely inclined.
translations.idiomatic: Ji An met him with a shallow bow, never a full prostration.

sentences.zh: 上方招文學儒者，上曰吾欲云云，默對曰：「陛下內多欲而外施仁義，奈何欲效唐、虞之治乎！」

translations.literal: The sovereign was recruiting men of letters and Confucians; the sovereign said he intended such-and-such; silently [An] replied, "Within, Your Majesty is full of desires, yet outwardly you dispense benevolence and righteousness—how then do you hope to imitate the government of Tang and Yu!"
translations.idiomatic: While the court was packing itself with literati and the emperor aired his lofty plans, Ji An answered flatly: "You are a cauldron of appetites inside while preaching benevolence and righteousness to the world—how do you expect to match the age of Yao and Shun?"

sentences.zh: 上怒，變色而罷朝。

translations.literal: The emperor grew angry, changed color, and dismissed court.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor's face darkened; he cut court short and stalked off.

sentences.zh: 公卿皆為黯懼。

translations.literal: All the dukes and ministers feared for An.
translations.idiomatic: The high ministers trembled for Ji An's safety.

sentences.zh: 上退，謂人曰：「甚矣，汲黯之戇心！」

translations.literal: When the sovereign withdrew he told someone, "Extreme indeed is Ji An's blunt stubbornness!"
translations.idiomatic: Later Wu muttered to an attendant, "Ji An's obstinacy is becoming intolerable!"

sentences.zh: 群臣或數黯，黯曰：「天子置公卿輔弼之臣，寧令從諛承意，陷主於不誼乎？

translations.literal: Some of the ministers rebuked An; An said, "The Son of Heaven sets up dukes and ministers as supporting ministers—is it to make them flatter and carry out his wishes, trapping the ruler in what is not right?
translations.idiomatic: When colleagues scolded him, Ji An shot back: "The throne appoints us as his brace and bracket—are we here to flatter him into moral disaster?

sentences.zh: 且已在其位，縱愛身，奈辱朝廷何！」

translations.literal: Moreover, once one holds the post, even if one cherishes one's person, how can one bear to disgrace the court!"
translations.idiomatic: And once you accept the seal, clinging to your skin while the dynasty is shamed is the greater cowardice!

sentences.zh: 黯多病，病且滿三月，上常賜告者數，終不愈。

translations.literal: An was often ill; his illness neared three months; the sovereign repeatedly granted him leave to recover, yet he never got better.
translations.idiomatic: Ji An was chronically sick; as three months neared, the emperor kept extending his sick leave, but he did not mend.

sentences.zh: 最後，嚴助為請告。

translations.literal: Finally Yan Zhu spoke for him requesting leave.
translations.idiomatic: At last Yan Zhu begged the throne on his behalf.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「汲黯何如人也？」

translations.literal: The sovereign said, "What sort of man is Ji An?"
translations.idiomatic: Wu asked, "What kind of man is Ji An really?"

sentences.zh: 曰：「使黯任職居官，亡以愈人，然至其輔少主守成，雖自謂賁、育弗能奪也。」

translations.literal: [Yan Zhu] said, "If you put An in office to handle affairs, he will not surpass others; yet when it comes to supporting a young ruler and guarding what is established, even men who call themselves Ben and Yu could not budge him."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Zhu replied: "Set him to routine administration and he seems ordinary; but charge him with guarding a young heir and the legacy of the house—even Ben and Yu could not shake his resolve."

sentences.zh: 上曰：「然。

translations.literal: The sovereign said, "So.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, "True enough."

sentences.zh: 古有社稷之臣，至如汲黯，近之矣！」

translations.literal: In antiquity there were ministers for the altars of soil and grain; as for Ji An, he approaches that!"
translations.idiomatic: The old histories speak of ministers who were the state itself—Ji An comes close to that ideal!

sentences.zh: 大將軍青侍中，上踞廁視之。

translations.literal: General-in-chief Wei Qing attended within; the sovereign squatted on the privy-stool to see him.
translations.idiomatic: When General Wei Qing waited on him in private, the emperor would receive him from the privy seat, legs sprawled.

sentences.zh: 丞相弘宴見，上或時不冠。

translations.literal: When Chancellor Hong had informal audience, the sovereign sometimes did not wear the cap.
translations.idiomatic: With Chancellor Gongsun Hong at a casual audience he sometimes went bareheaded.

sentences.zh: 至如見黯，不冠不見也。

translations.literal: But as for seeing An, without the cap he would not meet him.
translations.idiomatic: For Ji An he always donned full court dress.

sentences.zh: 上嘗坐武帳，黯前奏事，上不冠，望見黯，避帷中，使人可其奏。

translations.literal: The sovereign once sat in the military tent; An came forward to memorialize; the sovereign was not capped; seeing An from afar he hid behind the curtain and had someone approve his memorial.
translations.idiomatic: Once, seated in the war tent without his crown, he spotted Ji An approaching and slipped behind the screen, letting an attendant approve the memorial for him.

sentences.zh: 其見敬禮如此。

translations.literal: Thus was he respected and treated with ceremony.
translations.idiomatic: Such was the deference the emperor showed him.

sentences.zh: 張湯以更定律令為廷尉，黯質責湯於上前，曰：「公為正卿，上不能褒先帝之功業，下不能化天下之邪心，安國富民，使囹圄空虛，何空取高皇帝約束紛更之為？

translations.literal: Zhang Tang, having revised statutes and ordinances, became commandant of justice; An confronted and rebuked Tang before the sovereign, saying, "You are chief minister; above you cannot extol the late emperor's achievements, below you cannot transform the wicked hearts of the world, settle the state and enrich the people, or empty the prisons—why do you vainly take Gaozu's established rules and alter them in confusion?
translations.idiomatic: When Zhang Tang rewrote the code and became commandant of justice, Ji An cornered him in open court: "As chief minister you neither celebrate the founder's legacy nor reform public morals, bring peace to the realm, fill the granaries, nor clear the jails—why do you busy yourself tearing apart the statutes Gaozu left behind?

sentences.zh: 而公以此無種矣！」

translations.literal: And for this you will leave no posterity!"
translations.idiomatic: For that you will die without descendants!

sentences.zh: 黯時與湯論議，湯辯常在文深小苛，黯憤發，罵曰：「天下謂刀筆吏不可為公卿，果然。

translations.literal: When An debated policy with Tang, Tang's argumentation always lay in twisted wording and petty harshness; An flared with anger and cursed, saying, "The world says brush-and-knife clerks must not become dukes and ministers—it is indeed so.
translations.idiomatic: In policy debates Zhang Tang always hid behind legal hair-splitting; Ji An lost his temper and swore, "They say a clerk should never rise to high office—how true.

sentences.zh: 必湯也，令天下重足而立，仄目而視矣！」

translations.literal: If it must be Tang, he will make all under heaven stand with feet together and look sideways!"
translations.idiomatic: Let men like Zhang Tang win, and the whole empire will walk on tiptoe and glance sideways in fear!

sentences.zh: 是時，漢方征匈奴，招懷四夷。

translations.literal: At that time Han was just campaigning against the Xiongnu and winning over the four Yi.
translations.idiomatic: The Han was then deep in the Xiongnu wars and courting the border tribes.

sentences.zh: 黯務少事，間常言與胡和親，毋起兵。

translations.literal: An strove for few affairs and often spoke of peace and kinship with the Hu, not raising troops.
translations.idiomatic: Ji An pushed for less government and urged marriage alliances with the steppe instead of new campaigns.

sentences.zh: 上方鄉儒術，尊公孫弘，及事益多，吏民巧。

translations.literal: The sovereign was turning toward Confucian learning, honored Gongsun Hong, and as affairs grew more numerous, clerks and commoners grew crafty.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor meanwhile embraced Confucianism, elevated Gongsun Hong, and as business multiplied, officials and people grew expert at evasion.

sentences.zh: 上分別文法，湯等數奏決讞以幸。

translations.literal: The sovereign distinguished textual niceties in the law; Tang and others repeatedly memorialized verdicts to win favor.
translations.idiomatic: Wu delighted in legal exegesis; Zhang Tang and his circle fed him case after case to curry favor.

sentences.zh: 而黯常毀儒，面觸弘等徒懷詐飾智以阿人主取容，而刀筆之吏專深文巧詆，陷人於罔，以自為功。

translations.literal: But An often disparaged Confucians; to the face he attacked Hong and the rest for harboring deceit and displaying cleverness to fawn on the ruler and win accommodation, while brush-and-knife clerks specialized in twisted texts and clever slanders, trapping people in nets to make their own merit.
translations.idiomatic: Ji An, meanwhile, trashed the Ru scholars and accused Hong to his face of faking wisdom to please the throne, while clerks twisted the code to frame the innocent and pad their résumés.

sentences.zh: 上愈益貴弘、湯，弘、湯心疾黯，雖上亦不說也，欲誅之以事。

translations.literal: The sovereign came more and more to esteem Hong and Tang; Hong and Tang inwardly hated An—though the sovereign too was displeased—and wished to execute him on some pretext.
translations.idiomatic: Wu prized Hong and Tang all the more; they loathed Ji An, and the emperor himself cooled—yet all three looked for a charge that would let them kill him.

sentences.zh: 弘為丞相，乃言上曰：「右內史界部中多貴人宗室，難治，非素重臣弗能任，請徙黯。」

translations.literal: Hong being chancellor, then spoke to the sovereign, saying, "The territory of the right metropolitan superintendent has many nobles and imperial clansmen; it is hard to govern; unless it is a minister of long-standing weight he cannot bear the burden—I beg to transfer An."
translations.idiomatic: As chancellor, Gongsun Hong told the emperor that the right capital district swarmed with nobles and imperial kin, needed an iron hand, and ought to be given to Ji An.

sentences.zh: 為右內史數歲，官事不廢。

translations.literal: As right metropolitan superintendent for several years, official business was not neglected.
translations.idiomatic: Ji An held the post for years without letting administration slide.

sentences.zh: 大將軍青既益尊，姊為皇后，然黯與亢禮。

translations.literal: After General-in-chief Qing grew more honored and his elder sister became empress, yet An treated him with the etiquette of equals.
translations.idiomatic: Even after Wei Qing's star rose and his sister became empress, Ji An refused to grovel.

sentences.zh: 或說黯曰：「自天子欲令群臣下大將軍，大將軍尊貴，誠重，君不可以不拜。」

translations.literal: Someone urged An, saying, "The Son of Heaven wishes the ministers to rank below the general-in-chief; the general-in-chief is honored and weighty—in truth you cannot but bow."
translations.idiomatic: Friends warned him: "The court wants everyone below the general-in-chief; his rank is real—you really should prostrate yourself."

sentences.zh: 黯曰：「夫以大將軍有揖客，反不重耶？」

translations.literal: An said, "That the general-in-chief has retainers who receive only an incline—does that not show his weight?"
translations.idiomatic: Ji An retorted: "If the general-in-chief accepts a bow from a man who only inclines, does that not prove his stature?"

sentences.zh: 大將軍聞，愈賢黯，數請問以朝廷所疑，遇黯加於平日。

translations.literal: When the general-in-chief heard, he thought An more worthy, repeatedly asked him about doubts in court policy, and treated An with greater regard than in ordinary times.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Qing heard this, respected him the more, and sought his counsel on state questions, honoring him above his usual circle.

sentences.zh: 淮南王謀反，憚黯，曰：「黯好直諫，守節死義；

translations.literal: The king of Huainan plotted rebellion and feared An, saying, "An loves straight remonstrance and guards integrity unto death;
translations.idiomatic: The king of Huainan, plotting revolt, named Ji An as a danger: "He lives for blunt remonstrance and would die before betraying principle;

sentences.zh: 至說公孫弘等，如發蒙耳。」

translations.literal: as for persuading Gongsun Hong and the rest, it would be like lifting a blind."
translations.idiomatic: winning over men like Gongsun Hong would be child's play by comparison.

sentences.zh: 上既數征匈奴有功，黯言益不用。

translations.literal: Once the sovereign had repeatedly scored successes against the Xiongnu, An's words were less heeded.
translations.idiomatic: After the northern campaigns began to succeed, nobody listened to Ji An's protests.

sentences.zh: 始黯列九卿矣，而公孫弘、張湯為小吏。

translations.literal: At first An ranked with the nine ministers while Gongsun Hong and Zhang Tang were petty clerks.
translations.idiomatic: Ji An had been a nine-minister man when Hong and Tang were still clerks.

sentences.zh: 及弘、湯稍貴，與黯同位，黯又非毀弘、湯。

translations.literal: When Hong and Tang gradually grew noble and shared rank with An, An again condemned and disparaged Hong and Tang.
translations.idiomatic: When they rose to his level, he still spoke of them with contempt.

sentences.zh: 已而弘至丞相，封侯，湯御史大夫，黯時丞史皆與同列，或尊用過之。

translations.literal: In time Hong reached chancellor and was enfeoffed as marquis; Tang became imperial counsellor; at that time An's assistants and clerks were of the same rank, some honored and employed above him.
translations.idiomatic: Soon Hong was chancellor and a marquis, Tang imperial counsellor, while Ji An's old subordinates passed him in rank.

sentences.zh: 黯褊心，不能無少望，見上，言曰：「陛下用群臣如積薪耳，後來者居上。」

translations.literal: An's heart was narrow; he could not but harbor some resentment; seeing the sovereign he said, "Your Majesty employs the ministers like stacking firewood—the later arrivals sit on top."
translations.idiomatic: Bitterness ate at him; he told the emperor, "You stack your ministers like firewood—the last logs tossed on end up on top."

sentences.zh: 黯罷，上曰：「人果不可以無學，觀汲黯之言，日益甚矣。」

translations.literal: When An withdrew the sovereign said, "Indeed a man cannot lack learning; observe Ji An's words—they grow daily more extreme!"
translations.idiomatic: After Ji An left, Wu remarked, "A man really must read his books—listen to Ji An now; he grows cruder by the day."

sentences.zh: 居無何，匈奴渾邪王帥眾來降，漢發車二萬乘。

translations.literal: Before long the Xiongnu Hunye king led his people to surrender; Han requisitioned twenty thousand chariots.
translations.idiomatic: Not long after, the Hunye king came in with his tribes and the court ordered twenty thousand wagons to meet him.

sentences.zh: 縣官亡錢，從民貰馬。

translations.literal: The treasury had no money and borrowed horses from the people.
translations.idiomatic: The exchequer was empty, so the government borrowed horses from households.

sentences.zh: 民或匿馬，馬不具。

translations.literal: Some of the people hid their horses and the horses were not complete.
translations.idiomatic: Citizens hid their animals and the convoy could not be filled.

sentences.zh: 上怒，欲斬長安令。

translations.literal: The emperor grew angry and wished to execute the magistrate of Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: In a fury the emperor threatened to behead the magistrate of Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 黯曰：「長安令亡罪，獨斬臣黯，民乃肯出馬。

translations.literal: An said, "The magistrate of Chang'an is without guilt; only execute your servant An, and then the people will be willing to bring out horses.
translations.idiomatic: Ji An said, "The magistrate of Chang'an is innocent; behead me instead, and the citizens will surrender their horses."

sentences.zh: 且匈奴畔其主而降漢，徐以縣次傳之，何至令天下騷動，罷中國，甘心夷狄之人乎！」

translations.literal: Moreover the Xiongnu rebelled against their lord and surrendered to Han—slowly convey them relay by relay from county to county; how did it reach the point of throwing all under heaven into turmoil, exhausting the central states, and making people content to serve barbarians!"
translations.idiomatic: The tribes broke with their chieftain to join us—they could have been moved post by post, county by county. Why convulse the realm, drain the heartland, and treat them like pampered guests of honor?

sentences.zh: 上默然。

translations.literal: The sovereign was silent.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said nothing.

sentences.zh: 後渾邪王至，賈人與市者，坐當死五百餘人。

translations.literal: Later when the Hunye king arrived, merchants who traded with them—more than five hundred were sentenced to death.
translations.idiomatic: When Hunye arrived, over five hundred traders who had dealt with him were condemned to die.

sentences.zh: 黯入，請間，見高門，曰：「夫匈奴攻當路塞，絕和親，中國舉兵誅之，死傷不可勝計，而費以巨萬百數。

translations.literal: An entered, requested a private audience, saw him at Gaomen, and said, "When the Xiongnu attacked the strategic passes and severed peace and kinship, the central states raised troops to punish them—dead and wounded beyond counting, and expense reckoned in hundreds of millions.
translations.idiomatic: Ji An forced a private audience at Gaomen and said, "When the nomads seized the frontier passes and broke the marriage treaties, Han raised armies until the butcher's bill and the treasury toll ran beyond reckoning."

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為陛下得胡人，皆以為奴婢，賜從軍死者家；

translations.literal: In my foolish view I thought that when Your Majesty obtained Hu people you would make them all slaves and bondmaids and bestow them on the families of those who died in the army;
translations.idiomatic: I assumed you would enslave the captives and hand them to the kin of the fallen,

sentences.zh: 鹵獲，因與之，以謝天下，塞百姓之心。

translations.literal: and battle booty—thereby give it to them—to give satisfaction to all under heaven and assuage the common people.
translations.idiomatic: or distribute the spoils to the same end—to balance the scales for the empire and quiet public resentment.

sentences.zh: 今縱不能，渾邪帥數萬之眾來，虛府庫賞賜，發良民侍養，若奉驕子。

translations.literal: Now even if you cannot do that, Hunye leading tens of thousands to come—you empty the treasuries in rewards, dispatch good commoners to wait on and nurture them, as if serving a spoiled child.
translations.idiomatic: Instead you drain the vaults to reward them, press good farmers into service as their valets, and coddle them like spoiled heirs.

sentences.zh: 愚民安知市買長安中而文吏繩以為闌出財物如邊關乎？

translations.literal: How should ignorant commoners know that buying in Chang'an would have clerkly officials bind them as illegally exporting goods like at the frontier passes?
translations.idiomatic: How was a shopkeeper in Chang'an to guess that a street purchase would be twisted into 'smuggling goods past the border,' the way clerks treat frontier trade?

sentences.zh: 陛下縱不能得匈奴之贏以謝天下，又以微文殺無知者五百餘人，臣竊為陛下弗取也。」

translations.literal: Though Your Majesty could not take Xiongnu spoils to give satisfaction to all under heaven, you further use fine-print statutes to kill more than five hundred who did not know—privately I deem this unworthy of Your Majesty."
translations.idiomatic: You gain no Xiongnu plunder to appease the people, yet you kill five hundred innocents on legal technicalities—I cannot call that worthy of you.

sentences.zh: 上弗許，曰：「吾久不聞汲黯之言，今又復妄發矣。」

translations.literal: The sovereign did not grant it, saying, "I have long not heard Ji An's words; now he has again shot off rashly."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor waved it off: "I have missed Ji An's voice—but there he goes again, sounding off without restraint."

sentences.zh: 後數月，黯坐小法，會赦，免官。

translations.literal: Several months later An was charged with a minor legal offense; there happened to be an amnesty; he was dismissed from office.
translations.idiomatic: Months later Ji An caught a petty legal charge; a general amnesty spared him, but he lost his post.

sentences.zh: 於是黯隱於田園者數年。

translations.literal: Thereupon An hid in fields and gardens for several years.
translations.idiomatic: He withdrew to his estate for several years.

sentences.zh: 會更立五銖錢，民多盜鑄錢者，楚地尤甚。

translations.literal: There happened to be a change establishing the five-zhu coin; the people often counterfeited coins; Chu territory was especially severe.
translations.idiomatic: When the new five-zhu coin was issued, illicit minting spread, worst of all through Chu.

sentences.zh: 上以為淮陽，楚地之郊也，召黯拜為淮陽太守。

translations.literal: The sovereign thought Huaiyang lay on the marches of Chu territory, summoned An, and appointed him governor of Huaiyang.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor judged Huaiyang the gateway into Chu and summoned Ji An to serve as its governor.

sentences.zh: 黯伏謝不受印綬，詔數強予，然後奉詔。

translations.literal: An knelt and declined, refusing to accept the seal-ribbon; the edict repeatedly forced it on him; then he obeyed the edict.
translations.idiomatic: Ji An kowtowed and tried to refuse the seal until repeated edicts left him no choice but to accept.

sentences.zh: 召上殿，黯泣曰：「臣自以為填溝壑，不復見陛下，不意陛下復收之。

translations.literal: Summoned to ascend the hall, An wept and said, "I had thought I would fill ditches and gullies and never again see Your Majesty; I did not expect Your Majesty would take me back.
translations.idiomatic: Called before the throne in tears, he said, "I was sure I would die in obscurity and never look on your face again—I never dreamed you would call me back."

sentences.zh: 臣常有狗馬之心，今病，力不能任郡事。

translations.literal: I always had the heart of a dog or horse; now I am ill and lack strength to bear commandery affairs.
translations.idiomatic: My loyalty is still that of your old hound, but I am sick and no longer fit to run a commandery.

sentences.zh: 臣願為中郎，出入禁闥，補過拾遺，臣之願也。」

translations.literal: I beg to serve as gentleman of the palace, entering and leaving the inner gates, mending faults and picking up oversights—that is my wish."
translations.idiomatic: Let me serve as a palace gentleman instead—passing through the inner gates, catching small slips before they grow—that is all I dare ask.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「君薄淮陽邪？

translations.literal: The sovereign said, "Do you look down on Huaiyang?"
translations.idiomatic: "Do you think Huaiyang beneath you?"

sentences.zh: 吾今召君矣。

translations.literal: I have now summoned you.
translations.idiomatic: I have brought you here for a reason.

sentences.zh: 顧淮陽吏民不相得，吾徒得君重，臥而治之。」

translations.literal: Only Huaiyang's clerks and people do not get along—I merely need your weight; lie down and govern it."
translations.idiomatic: The place is torn between its magistrates and the people; I need nothing but the weight of your name—govern it even from your couch if you must.

sentences.zh: 黯既辭，過大行李息，曰：「黯棄逐居郡，不得與朝廷議矣。

translations.literal: After An took his leave he passed Grand Herald Li Xi and said, "Cast off to dwell in a commandery, I cannot join court deliberations.
translations.idiomatic: On his way out Ji An stopped Grand Herald Li Xi: "Banished to the provinces, I shall no longer speak in council."

sentences.zh: 然御史大夫湯智足以距諫，詐足以飾非，非肯正為天下言，專阿主意。

translations.literal: Yet Imperial Counsellor Tang's cleverness is sufficient to reject remonstrance, his deceit sufficient to cover faults; he is not willing to speak straight for the empire but solely fawns on the ruler's intent.
translations.idiomatic: But Zhang Tang is clever enough to block every remonstrance and slick enough to varnish every fault; he will not speak for the realm, only stroke the emperor's mood.

sentences.zh: 主意所不欲，因而毀之；

translations.literal: What the ruler's intent dislikes, he thereupon slanders;
translations.idiomatic: What the throne dislikes, he trashes;

sentences.zh: 主意所欲，因而譽之。

translations.literal: what the ruler's intent desires, he thereupon praises.
translations.idiomatic: what it favors, he praises.

sentences.zh: 好興事，舞文法，內懷詐以御主心，外挾賊吏以為重。

translations.literal: He loves to raise affairs and twist the letter of the law; inwardly he harbors deceit to steer the ruler's heart; outwardly he relies on vicious clerks to make his weight.
translations.idiomatic: He stirs up business, twists statutes, plays on your mind from within, and leans on cruel underlings from without.

sentences.zh: 公列九卿不早言之何？

translations.literal: You ranked among the nine ministers—why did you not speak early?
translations.idiomatic: You sit among the nine ministers—why have you never called him out?

sentences.zh: 公與之俱受其戮矣！」

translations.literal: You will with him together face execution!"
translations.idiomatic: You will share his scaffold if you stay silent!

sentences.zh: 息畏湯，終不敢言。

translations.literal: Xi feared Tang and in the end did not dare speak.
translations.idiomatic: Li Xi feared Zhang Tang too much ever to repeat the warning.

sentences.zh: 黯居郡如其故治，淮陽政清。

translations.literal: An dwelt in the commandery governing as before; Huaiyang's government was pure.
translations.idiomatic: Ji An governed Huaiyang in his old style, and the region became a model of honest rule.

sentences.zh: 後張湯敗，上聞黯與息言，抵息罪。

translations.literal: Later when Zhang Tang fell, the sovereign heard what An had said to Xi and imposed punishment on Xi.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhang Tang fell, the emperor remembered Ji An's words to Li Xi and punished Li Xi for his silence.

sentences.zh: 令黯以諸侯相秩居淮陽。

translations.literal: He ordered An to dwell in Huaiyang at the salary rank of a chancellor to the feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: He let Ji An remain at Huaiyang on the salary scale of a feudal chancellor.

sentences.zh: 居淮陽十歲而卒。

translations.literal: He dwelt in Huaiyang ten years and died.
translations.idiomatic: He died there after ten years.

sentences.zh: 卒後，上以黯故，官其弟仁至九卿，子偃至諸侯相。

translations.literal: After his death, because of An the sovereign gave office to his younger brother Ren up to the nine ministers, and his son Yan became chancellor to the feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: After his death the emperor honored his memory by promoting his brother Ren among the nine ministers and his son Yan to a princely chancellorship.

sentences.zh: 黯姊子司馬安亦少與黯為太子洗馬。

translations.literal: An's elder sister's son Sima An had in youth together with An been groom of the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: His sister's son Sima An had once served with him as groom to the crown prince.

sentences.zh: 安文深巧善宦，四至九卿，以河南太守卒。

translations.literal: An was deep in texts, clever, skilled at office; four times he reached the nine ministers, and died as governor of Henan.
translations.idiomatic: Sima An was a subtle stylist and a born bureaucrat; he rose four times to the nine ministers and died as governor of Henan.

sentences.zh: 昆弟以安故，同時至二千石十人。

translations.literal: Cousins because of him at the same time reached two thousand piculs—ten persons.
translations.idiomatic: On his account as many as ten kinsmen held posts at the two-thousand-picul level at once.

sentences.zh: 濮陽段宏始事蓋侯信，信任宏，官亦再至九卿。

translations.literal: Duan Hong of Puyang at first served Marquis of Gai Xin; Xin trusted Hong; Hong's office also twice reached the nine ministers.
translations.idiomatic: Duan Hong of Puyang began as a follower of Marquis of Gai Xin, won his trust, and himself twice reached the nine ministers.

sentences.zh: 然衛人仕者皆嚴憚汲黯，出其下。

translations.literal: Yet among Wei natives who held office all strictly feared Ji An and ranked below him.
translations.idiomatic: Yet every official from Wei still stood in awe of Ji An and conceded him the first place.

sentences.zh: 鄭當時

translations.literal: Zheng Dangshi
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Dangshi

sentences.zh: 鄭當時字莊，陳人也。

translations.literal: Zheng Dangshi, courtesy name Zhuang, was a native of Chen.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Dangshi, courtesy name Zhuang, came from Chen commandery.

sentences.zh: 其先鄭君嘗事項籍，籍死而屬漢。

translations.literal: His ancestor Lord Zheng had once served Xiang Yu; when Yu died he attached to Han.
translations.idiomatic: His forebear Lord Zheng had served Xiang Ji; when Ji fell he transferred his allegiance to Han.

sentences.zh: 高祖令諸故項籍臣名籍，鄭君獨不奉詔。

translations.literal: Gaozu ordered all former ministers of Xiang Yu to name Yu [by his personal name as contempt]; Lord Zheng alone did not comply with the edict.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu ordered Xiang Yu's old retainers to refer to him by his personal name as a sign of submission; Lord Zheng alone refused the edict.

sentences.zh: 詔盡拜名籍者為大夫，而逐鄭君。

translations.literal: The edict appointed all who named Yu as grandees and expelled Lord Zheng.
translations.idiomatic: Those who obeyed were made grandees; Lord Zheng was banished.

sentences.zh: 鄭君死孝文時。

translations.literal: Lord Zheng died in Emperor Xiaowen's time.
translations.idiomatic: Lord Zheng died during the reign of Emperor Wen.

sentences.zh: 當時以任俠自喜，脫張羽於厄，聲聞梁、楚間。

translations.literal: Dangshi prided himself on knight-errantry and rescued Zhang Yu from peril; his reputation spread between Liang and Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Zhuang made a name as a knight-errant, once snatching Zhang Yu from mortal danger; his fame ran through Liang and Chu.

sentences.zh: 孝景時，為太子舍人。

translations.literal: Under Emperor Jing he was attendant to the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Jing he served as a gentleman-attendant to the crown prince.

sentences.zh: 每五日洗沐，常置驛馬長安諸郊，請謝賓客，夜以繼日，至明旦，常恐不遍。

translations.literal: Every five days on bath-leave he always stationed relay horses in the outskirts of Chang'an, inviting and thanking guests, night succeeding day until the next dawn, always fearing not to reach everyone.
translations.idiomatic: On each five-day leave he posted relay horses around Chang'an and spent nights and dawns hosting every guest he could, terrified of missing anyone.

sentences.zh: 當時好黃、老言，其慕長者，如恐不稱。

translations.literal: Dangshi loved Huang-Lao discourse; in admiring senior worthies he seemed to fear not matching them.
translations.idiomatic: He studied Huang-Lao texts and courted venerable men as though he could never honor them enough.

sentences.zh: 自見年少官薄，然其知友皆大父行，天下有名之士也。

translations.literal: Though he saw himself as young and of thin rank, his intimate friends were all of his grandfather's generation, famous gentlemen of the empire.
translations.idiomatic: Though young and low in rank, he befriended men a generation older—the most celebrated names of the day.

sentences.zh: 當時為大吏，戒門下：「客至，亡貴賤亡留門者。」

translations.literal: When Dangshi was a senior official he instructed his gate: "When guests arrive, whether noble or base, do not detain them at the gate."
translations.idiomatic: As a high official he ordered his porters: "Admit every caller, high or low, without keeping them waiting at the door."

sentences.zh: 執賓主之禮，以其貴下人。

translations.literal: He observed host-guest ritual and with his nobility humbled himself to others.
translations.idiomatic: He received them with full courtesy and let his own rank bow to theirs.

sentences.zh: 性廉，又不治產，卬奉賜給諸公。

translations.literal: By nature incorrupt and again not managing estates, he used salary and gifts to supply gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: He was honest, kept no landed fortune, and poured every stipend and gift into helping friends.

sentences.zh: 然其饋遺人，不過具器食。

translations.literal: Yet what he sent as gifts to others did not exceed ordinary vessels and food.
translations.idiomatic: His own presents to others never rose above a simple meal in plain ware.

sentences.zh: 每朝，候上間說，未嘗不言天下長者。

translations.literal: At each court session he waited for the sovereign's spare moments in speaking and never failed to mention senior worthies of the empire.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever he caught the emperor's ear he spoke of worthy men across the realm.

sentences.zh: 其推轂士及官屬丞史，誠有味其言也。

translations.literal: In recommending gentlemen and official subordinates, assistants and clerks, there was truly savor in what he said.
translations.idiomatic: The way he pushed chariots for scholars and for his own deputies had real conviction.

sentences.zh: 常引以為賢於己。

translations.literal: He constantly cited them as worthier than himself.
translations.idiomatic: He always praised them as better men than he was.

sentences.zh: 未嘗名吏，與官屬言，若恐傷之。

translations.literal: He never called clerks by name; when speaking with subordinates it was as if he feared to wound them.
translations.idiomatic: He never barked a clerk's name; with subordinates he spoke as though afraid to bruise them.

sentences.zh: 聞人之善言，進之上，唯恐後。

translations.literal: When he heard others' good words he advanced them to the sovereign, fearing only to be too late.
translations.idiomatic: Good words about others he relayed to the throne the same day, anxious not to lag.

sentences.zh: 山東諸公為此翕然稱鄭莊。

translations.literal: The great lords east of the mountains for this unanimously praised Zheng Zhuang.
translations.idiomatic: For this the great families east of the mountains honored him as Zheng Zhuang.

sentences.zh: 使視決河，自請治行五日。

translations.literal: Sent to inspect the river breach, he himself requested five days to arrange his traveling gear.
translations.idiomatic: Dispatched to inspect a Yellow River break, he asked five days to pack.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「吾聞鄭莊行，千里不繼糧，治行者何也？」

translations.literal: The sovereign said, "I have heard that when Zheng Zhuang travels he needs no successive supplies for a thousand li—what is this about arranging traveling gear?"
translations.idiomatic: "They say Zheng Zhuang can cross a thousand li without fresh provisions—why five days to get ready?"

sentences.zh: 然當時以朝，常趨和承意，不敢甚斥臧否。

translations.literal: Yet Dangshi at court always hurried to harmonize and carry out the sovereign's intent, not daring sharply to judge right and wrong.
translations.idiomatic: In open court, though, he hurried to agree with the emperor and never risked a hard verdict on right and wrong.

sentences.zh: 漢征匈奴，招四夷，天下費多，財用益屈。

translations.literal: Han campaigned against the Xiongnu and summoned the four Yi; expenses in the empire were many and fiscal resources grew ever strained.
translations.idiomatic: Han's wars against the Xiongnu and its courtship of the border peoples bled the treasury dry.

sentences.zh: 當時為大司農，任人賓客僦，入多逋負。

translations.literal: Dangshi was grand minister of agriculture; in letting people employ guests for hire-rent, income had many arrears.
translations.idiomatic: As grand minister of finance he let contractors use his clients as labor; the accounts fell deep into arrears.

sentences.zh: 司馬安為淮陽太守，發其事，當時在此陷罪，贖為庶人。

translations.literal: Sima An as governor of Huaiyang exposed the matter; Dangshi on this account fell into crime and ransomed himself to commoner status.
translations.idiomatic: Sima An, then governor of Huaiyang, exposed the scheme; Zheng Dangshi paid a ransom and was reduced to commoner.

sentences.zh: 頃之，守長史。

translations.literal: Before long he was acting chief clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Soon he was serving as chief clerk.

sentences.zh: 遷汝南太守，數歲，以官卒。

translations.literal: He was promoted governor of Runan; after several years he died in office.
translations.idiomatic: He rose to governor of Runan and died in that post a few years later.

sentences.zh: 昆弟以當時故，至二千石者六七人。

translations.literal: Cousins because of Dangshi reached two thousand piculs—six or seven persons.
translations.idiomatic: Six or seven of his kinsmen reached the two-thousand-picul rank on his coattails.

sentences.zh: 當時始與汲黯列為九卿，內行修。

translations.literal: Dangshi at first with Ji An was ranked among the nine ministers; his private conduct was cultivated.
translations.idiomatic: He and Ji An had once stood together among the nine ministers, both men of scrupulous private conduct.

sentences.zh: 兩人中廢，賓客益落。

translations.literal: The two men were dismissed midway; guests increasingly fell away.
translations.idiomatic: When both fell from power, their salons emptied.

sentences.zh: 當時死，家亡余財。

translations.literal: When Dangshi died, the household had no surplus property.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Dangshi died leaving no savings.

sentences.zh: 先是，下刲翟公為廷尉，賓客亦填門，及廢，門外可設爵羅。

translations.literal: Earlier, Lord Zhai Gong of Xiagui had been commandant of justice; guests also filled his gate; when dismissed, outside the gate one could set a net for sparrows.
translations.idiomatic: Long before, Lord Zhai of Xiagui served as commandant of justice with a crowded gate; once dismissed, sparrows could nest outside his door—so empty was it.

sentences.zh: 後復為廷尉，客欲往，翟公大署其門，曰：「一死一生，乃知交情；

translations.literal: Later he again became commandant of justice; guests wished to return; Lord Zhai wrote large on his gate: "One death one life—then you know the bonds of friendship;
translations.idiomatic: When he was reappointed, old friends crept back; Lord Zhai chalked a couplet on his gate: "Friendship shows its depth only between death and life;

sentences.zh: 一貧一富，乃知交態；

translations.literal: One poor, one rich—then you know how friendship behaves;
translations.idiomatic: Only between wealth and poverty do you see how friends really carry themselves;

sentences.zh: 一貴一賤，交情乃見。」

translations.literal: One honored, one despised—then the truth of friendship appears."
translations.idiomatic: only between honor and disgrace does friendship show its true face."

sentences.zh: 贊曰：張釋之之守法，馮唐之論將，汲黯之正直，鄭當時之推士，不如是，亦何以成名哉！

translations.literal: The appraisal says: Zhang Shizhi's guarding the law, Feng Tang's discourse on generals, Ji An's uprightness, Zheng Dangshi's recommending gentlemen—if not thus, how would they have made a name?
translations.idiomatic: The historian's verdict: without Zhang Shizhi's fidelity to statute, Feng Tang's counsel on commanders, Ji An's blunt spine, and Zheng Zhuang's zeal for talent, none of them would be remembered.

sentences.zh: 揚子以為孝文帝詘帝尊以信亞夫之軍，曷為不能用頗、牧？

translations.literal: Yang Xiong thought that when Emperor Xiaowen humbled imperial dignity to give faith to Zhou Yafu's army, how was it that he could not employ Po and Mu?
translations.idiomatic: Yang Xiong objected: Emperor Wen once bowed his imperial pride to trust Zhou Yafu's camp—how could the same man fail to use Lian Po and Li Mu?

sentences.zh: 彼將有激云爾。

translations.literal: That was something said with provocation—that is all.
translations.idiomatic: Feng Tang spoke in the heat of argument—that is all there is to it.
